questions.text: I rarely notice when someone phrases a disagreement as a question to soften it.
I believe that the energy I spend on myself comes back multiplied.
I research purchases carefully, comparing options and reading reviews before buying.
I hold the view that the ability to analyze suffering without being emotionally destabilized by it is the quality that enables truly strategic and impactful helping.
I hold the view that it's better to be forgotten and helpful than to be remembered and self-serving.
I feel that hesitation costs more than imperfect action.
I feel a deep sense of duty to use everything I have in service of others.
I pick up on the slight tightening of someone's jaw when they suppress a reaction.
I pick up on the implicit social contracts operating in different cultural contexts.
I tend to announce my decisions rather than propose them for feedback.
I walk into meetings ready to engage with whatever comes up, without advance preparation.
I feel that the moral significance of suffering is what makes my ethical framework coherent and actionable.
I am happy to pass the microphone to whoever wants it.
I feel that my emotional reactions often have a life of their own, beyond my conscious control.
I value predictability in my daily routine because it lets me focus on what matters.
I experience emotional pain as something sharp and physical, not abstract.
I believe that when suffering is brought to my attention, the question of whether to respond is already answered.
I find that emotional events don't leave a long residue; I process and move on.
